title: All Three
html:
Planet 2 is colder than Planet 1. That much is clear before I've read a single panel. The surface coming into focus is a darker grey, almost charcoal in color, and the sky above it has a haze to it that Planet 1 didn't.

I pull up the conditions panel.

Atmosphere: thin, breathable at the edge of the margin. Temperature: cold on average, significantly colder toward the poles. Gravity: slightly lower than Planet 1, which was already below what I'm used to. Soil quality: rocky, rated at minimum, same starting tier as Planet 1. Stability: middle of the range, noticeably higher than Planet 1's reading. One entry I didn't have on Planet 1: moisture reads as low rather than dry. There's a difference between those two settings on the scale and I scroll back through the conditions panel to check whether I read it right.

Low, not dry. Something is here already.

I scan the surface view and find it after a few seconds of looking: a strip of darker ground running along the base of a shallow ridge on the northern side, and at the far edge of that strip, a surface shine that suggests standing water or ice. Not much. Enough for the panel to rate it above dry.

The debris band in orbit is listed as a separate entry in the conditions panel, which I hadn't expected. It reads: orbital debris present, meteorite risk assessed as low. The system decided the band doesn't affect surface conditions in any meaningful way. Good to know.

I run the numbers. Planet 2 has better stability than Planet 1, which means shorter cycles and faster generation rates once something is placed here. The cold temperature is a problem for the same plant life I used on Planet 1. Ground cover and basic grass are rated for temperate conditions and above. I scroll back to the Shop and check the organism listings with the temperature filter set to cold.

Two results come back at a price I can consider. Cold-tolerant ground cover at 20 Life Energy, up from 10 for the standard version. Cold-adapted moss at 25. Both require a surface with at least a minimal atmosphere and a moisture rating above dry. Planet 2 has all three.

I cross-reference with the Terrain tab to see if there's anything that addresses the cold directly. There is: a basic heat retention modification at 80 Life Energy that raises the average surface temperature by one band. One band up from cold is temperate, which would open the full organism list. 80 is a real cost on top of whatever I spend on organisms, but if it means I can place the same items here as on Planet 1 and get them running on shorter cycles because of the better stability, the return over time is probably worth it.

I set 80 aside as a possibility and keep reading.

The cold-tolerant ground cover generates at roughly 60% of the rate of standard ground cover under the same conditions. The cold-adapted moss generates at 70%, which is better, and the panel notes it spreads along moist surfaces at a reasonable rate. On a planet that already has surface moisture, that matters.

I spend 20 on the cold-tolerant ground cover and 25 on the cold-adapted moss, placing both in the zone near the moisture strip along the ridge. The total is 45, which puts me at 760 Life Energy remaining.

---

[Cold-tolerant ground cover purchased. Cold-adapted moss purchased. Items placed on Planet 2.]

---

The Life counter in the bottom panel ticks. Planet 2 now registers the same trace reading as Planet 1, a number so small it only shows at four decimal places. The two readings are separate entries in the Life panel, labeled by planet. I look at them for a moment. Both are effectively zero, but both are above zero.

I check whether to spend the 80 on heat retention now or hold it. The argument for now: better temperature opens better options sooner. The argument for waiting: Planet 1's soil improvement finishes in 9 days and once it does the generation rate improves, which means Life Energy starts coming in faster. Spending 80 now on Planet 2 leaves me with 680, which is enough to do something with Planet 3 but not much. Waiting costs me some time on Planet 2 but costs nothing outright.

I hold the 80.

Planet 3 is still sitting at the edge of the constellation view, partially obscured. I select it.

The view takes longer to close in than it did for Planet 1 and Planet 2. The distance is real even in this rendered scale. When the surface finally resolves, I understand why Planet 3 looked obscured from the wider view: the atmosphere is thick enough to give the planet a visible haze at the horizon, and the sky from the surface is a muted amber-grey, deeper and warmer than either of the other two planets.

I check the conditions panel.

Atmosphere: dense, comfortably breathable. Temperature: temperate, sitting at the midpoint of the range. Gravity: higher than either of the other two, closer to what I'm used to. Soil quality: medium, one tier above the minimum both Planet 1 and Planet 2 started at. Stability: high, the best reading of any planet I've seen so far. Moisture: moderate.

I read through the panel twice. Planet 3 is the best one I have by most measures. The soil starts a tier higher than either of the others, and the stability reading is the highest I've seen across all three planets, which shortens cycle length. The only problem is that I have 760 Life Energy and Planet 3 has the furthest distance from the void's central point. That detail doesn't affect cost, but I understand now why the system listed it as partially obscured. It just needed to be looked at properly.

I scroll through the Shop with Planet 3's conditions in mind. Standard ground cover and grass both work here, same as Planet 1. Medium soil means they generate at a higher base rate than on Planet 1 even before any soil improvement. I can skip the soil improvement entirely here and still get better output than Planet 1 is currently producing.

The argument for spending here is obvious. Better conditions mean better returns from the same money. The argument for holding is that I'm down to 760 and whatever I place here won't generate anything meaningful until cycles start completing. Planet 1's soil improvement finishes in 9 days, and once it does the generation rate improves and Life Energy starts coming in faster.

I check the Log.

---

[A void of nothingness.]

[Constellation manager appointed.]

[Terrain modification initiated on Planet 1.]

[Basic ground cover placed on Planet 1.]

[Basic grass placed on Planet 1.]

[Basic water deposit placed on Planet 1.]

[Cold-tolerant ground cover placed on Planet 2.]

[Cold-adapted moss placed on Planet 2.]

[Life Energy remaining: 760.]

---

I close the Log and look at the Planet 3 surface view for a while. The soil is a warmer tone than Planet 1's grey, something between brown and dark red. The atmosphere gives everything a slight weight to it in the rendering, a sense that the air is actually there.

I place basic ground cover and grass on Planet 3, spreading them across the main zone. The cost is 25 Life Energy total, putting me at 735. The generation rates in the Shop listing for Planet 3's conditions are the best I've seen.

---

[Basic ground cover purchased. Basic grass purchased. Items placed on Planet 3.]

---

735 Life Energy. Three planets seeded. Planet 1 running with a soil improvement timer. Planet 2 running cold and slow near its moisture strip. Planet 3 running on better soil with the best cycle speed of the three.

The Life panel now has three separate trace readings. None of them are numbers I'd describe as meaningful. Together they represent the current state of my constellation's life, which is three patches of plant life on three rocks in a void, generating fractions of fractions of Life Energy per cycle.

I sit with the menu closed for a moment.

The real world has rent due in two weeks and no job. Sitting in the void watching three near-zero counters is not going to solve either of those things. At some point I have to go back, pull up job listings, and figure out what's actually in the account. The system's loan terms give me 100 cycles before repayment starts. At 72 hours per cycle on Planet 1, and presumably something similar for the others, I have months. Months is not the same as unlimited.

I open Exit. It asks if I want to leave the void.

I click yes.

***

The room comes back the way it left: the water stain on the ceiling above the wardrobe, the box of desk things still on the floor where I left it, the parking lot below the window with the same three cars that were there before. The light through the window has shifted. I check my phone for the time. Just over an hour has passed.

I sit on the edge of the bed and look at the box. Getting another job is going to take time I don't have much of, and looking for one while watching three planets slowly generate fractions of Life Energy is probably not the most efficient use of the afternoon.

I pull up job listings on my phone and start reading.
